attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic for a routine health review. He has treated hypertension and takes perindopril. He smokes five to 10 cigarettes per day, though he admits he previously smoked much more, and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works as a banker. His father had a myocardial infarction at the age of 75 years.

On examination, he appears well with a pulse rate of 72/min, blood pressure 150/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His body mass index is 27 kg/m². There are no abnormal findings on cardiovascular or respiratory examination.

His fasting lipid profile shows total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.5) and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 1.0 mmol/L (1.0-2.0). His estimated absolute cardiovascular risk using the Australian cardiovascular disease risk calculator is 12% (high) over five years.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the next most appropriate step?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has been identified as having high cardiovascular (CV) risk (12% over five years) using the Australian cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk calculator. The calculator incorporates multiple risk factors, including age, sex, smoking status, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and diabetes status. With a risk ≥10% over five years, this patient falls into the high-risk category requiring immediate intervention.

Discuss statin therapy is the most appropriate next step. Australian guidelines recommend that all patients with high CV risk should be offered lipid-lowering therapy with statins, regardless of their baseline cholesterol levels. This discussion should include the benefits of risk reduction, potential side effects, and the importance of lifestyle modifications alongside medication.

Coronary artery calcium scoring is not appropriate in this case. According to Australian guidelines, coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring could be considered when the calculated cardiovascular risk is low or intermediate and treatment decisions remain uncertain. CAC scoring may help reclassify risk when additional risk factors not captured by the calculator are present, or when further information would help inform shared decision-making about therapy. However, this patient already has a high-risk classification (12% over five years), making further risk stratification unnecessary. The CAC score would not change the recommendation for statin therapy and would only delay treatment initiation.

CT coronary angiography is not indicated for asymptomatic patients undergoing CV risk assessment. This invasive imaging test is typically reserved for patients with symptoms suggestive of coronary artery disease or those with equivocal stress test results. In an asymptomatic high-risk patient, the focus should be on risk factor modification rather than anatomical assessment of coronary arteries.

Discuss empagliflozin therapy would not be appropriate as first-line therapy in this context. While SGLT2 inhibitors like empagliflozin have shown CV benefits, they are primarily indicated for patients with type 2 diabetes or established CVD. This patient has no mention of diabetes, and statins remain the first-line therapy for primary prevention in high-risk patients without diabetes.

Discuss alirocumab therapy is not appropriate as initial therapy. Alirocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor that is reserved for specific situations such as familial hypercholesterolaemia, patients who cannot achieve target cholesterol levels despite maximal statin therapy, or those who are statin intolerant. It is not indicated as first-line therapy for primary prevention, even in high-risk patients. The cost and need for subcutaneous administration also make it unsuitable as initial therapy when statins have not yet been tried.
learningPoints: Patients with high cardiovascular risk (≥10% over five years) should be offered statin therapy as part of comprehensive risk reduction.
Coronary artery calcium scoring can be considered when cardiovascular risk is low or intermediate and treatment decisions are uncertain.
references: Australian absolute cardiovascular disease risk calculator. In: National Vascular Disease Prevention Alliance [online]. Accessed 2025 Jun 25. Available at: https://www.cvdcheck.org.au/calculator

Chua A, Blankstein R, Ko B. Coronary artery calcium in primary prevention. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2020;49:464-9. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2020/august/coronary-artery-calcium-in-primary-prevention

Jennings GL, Audehm R, Bishop W, Chow CK, Liaw ST, Liew D, Linton SM. National Heart Foundation of Australia: position statement on coronary artery calcium scoring for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in Australia. Med J Aust. 2021 May;214(9):434-439. Available at: https://doi.org/10.5694/mja2.51039

Nelson MR, Banks E, Brown A, Chow CK, Peiris DP, Stocks NP, Davies Ao R, Raffoul N, Kalman L, Bradburn E, Jennings G. 2023 Australian guideline for assessing and managing cardiovascular disease risk. Med J Aust. 2024 May 20;220(9):482-490. Available at: https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2024/220/9/2023-australian-guideline-assessing-and-managing-cardiovascular-disease-risk

Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. First do no harm: A guide to choosing wisely in general practice [Internet]. East Melbourne, Vic: RACGP; 2016 [cited 2025 Aug 8]. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/FSDEDEV/media/documents/Clinical%20Resources/First-do-no-harm.pdf

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
